coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient visible/near-infrared light photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic wastewater over 3D yolk-shell ZnFe2O4 supported 0D carbon dots with up-conversion property

coredata.dc:description: The development of effective visible and near-infrared photocatalysts is highly promising in the current field of photocatalysis. Herein, carbon dots/ZnFe2O4 (CDs/ZFO) with coating zero dimensional (0D) CDs on the surface of three dimensional (3D) yolk-shell ZFO spheres was designed and synthesized via a self-templated solvothermal method. The as-prepared CDs/ZFO composites displayed outstanding visible and near-infrared photocatalytic degradation activity of tetracycline (TC), and the optimal 3% CDs/ZFO sample with loading 3% (mass) CDs displayed the highest photocatalytic TC degradation ability under visible light (79.5% within 120 min) and near-infrared light (41% within 120 min). The enhancement of photocatalytic activity for CDs/ZFO composite is mainly ascribed to the fact that 0D/3D yolk-shell CDs/ZFO structure not only effectively reflect the incident light to increase the utilization efficiency of solar light, but also utilize the up-conversion photoluminescence and electronic conductivity properties of CDs to broaden sunlight absorption range and promote separation and transfer of electron-hole pairs.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient visible/near-infrared light photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic wastewater over 3D yolk-shell ZnFe2O4 supported 0D carbon dots with up-conversion property

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of effective visible and near-infrared photocatalysts is highly promising in the current field of photocatalysis. Herein, carbon dots/ZnFe2O4 (CDs/ZFO) with coating zero dimensional (0D) CDs on the surface of three dimensional (3D) yolk-shell ZFO spheres was designed and synthesized via a self-templated solvothermal method. The as-prepared CDs/ZFO composites displayed outstanding visible and near-infrared photocatalytic degradation activity of tetracycline (TC), and the optimal 3% CDs/ZFO sample with loading 3% (mass) CDs displayed the highest photocatalytic TC degradation ability under visible light (79.5% within 120 min) and near-infrared light (41% within 120 min). The enhancement of photocatalytic activity for CDs/ZFO composite is mainly ascribed to the fact that 0D/3D yolk-shell CDs/ZFO structure not only effectively reflect the incident light to increase the utilization efficiency of solar light, but also utilize the up-conversion photoluminescence and electronic conductivity properties of CDs to broaden sunlight absorption range and promote separation and transfer of electron-hole pairs.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants is a potential strategies of utilizing solar energy for achieving the effective treatment of the sewage [1–8]. Currently, the researchers are still focusing on ultraviolet/visible light response photocatalysts, with little research in the near-infrared light driven photocatalyst in the field of photocatalysis [9–13]. Nevertheless, although numerous efforts have been devoted to develop effective ultraviolet/visible photocatalyst for photocatalytic degradation, several drawbacks still remain, such as poor utilization efficiency of sunlight, high cost and difficult to recycle [14,15]. Thus, the development of photocatalysts with the advantages of high-efficiency, stable, cost-effective, broad solar spectrum response range, and easily recyclable is of great significance to promote the photocatalytic technology from basic research to practical application.

Recently, ZnFe2O4 (ZFO), as n-type semiconductor photocatalyst, has received extensive attention and research owing to the advantages of narrow band gap (∼1.9 eV), visible-light response, low-cost, stability and innocuity [16–18]. Furthermore, because of the unique structural features of ZFO, in which Zn2+ and Fe3+ located at the tetrahedra and octahedra positions, respectively, giving ZFO photocatalyst outstanding magnetic recovery potential, which helps to reducing the risk of sample loss and secondary contamination during the recovery process [19–21]. Nevertheless, the practical application potential of ZFO photocatalyst is limited due to the massive aggregation of nanoparticles, the limited response of the near-infrared light region, and rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs [22,23]. Consequently, the rational design and construction of ZFO-based composites to further improve photocatalytic activity through appropriate modification remains a significant challenge.

Microstructure is known to be an important factor affecting the photocatalytic capability, and a regular shape and proper size may lead to more satisfactory photocatalytic properties compared to irregular structures [24]. In particular, the unique three-dimensional (3D) yolk-shell structure has a larger specific area, stronger activity, reflecting incident light, and improving the utilization efficiency of photons [25,26]. For example, Wang et al. [27] reported that UCNPs/Zn x Cd1−
x S yolk-shell nanoparticles exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance for degradation towards rhodamine B (Rh B), which is attributed to enhanced utilization efficiency of incident visible light utilization and faster energy transfer. Jiang et al. [28] synthesized g-C3N4 modified hierarchical yolk-shell TiO2 spheres and applied in water pollution treatment, and the results reveal that the multiple reflections of lights in this unique structure could increase the absorbance of heterojunction, leading to improve photocatalytic efficiency over other TiO2 with conventional structure. Thus, the ZFO photocatalyst with the yolk-shell structure is expected to have a higher photocatalytic activity than the irregular structure ZFO photocatalyst.

In addition to optimize the internal structure of the photocatalyst, the external surface modification of the photocatalyst has been shown to be one of the most effective methods to further improve the photocatalytic performance [29]. Carbon dots (CDs) are considered as the promising zero dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial due to the fascinating advantage of photoinduced electron transfer property, non-toxicity, suitable size (2–10 nm), large production volume and low budget [30–32]. Encouragingly, the loading of CDs on the surface of photocatalysts will further expand the sunlight utilization range of the photocatalyst from the visible to the near-infrared light region owing to the fascinating up-conversion photoluminescence properties, which can convert longer wavelength light into shorter wavelength light, thus enhancing the utilization of solar light [33,34]. Recently, the construction of 0D/3D contact surface in semiconductor photocatalysts has been confirmed to be a promising modification to enhance the photocatalytic activity, which may be ascribed to the following two reasons: (i) the 3D materials will provide adequate specific surface area in contact with 0D materials, which not only facilitates the migration of photon-generated carriers between 0D and 3D materials, but also hinder the large-scale aggregation of 0D materials [35,36]; (ii) 0D materials can provide additional activity sites on the surface of photocatalysts, resulting in more adequate response with pollutions [37,38].

Inspired by the afore-mentioned unique features, 0D CDs modified 3D yolk-shell ZFO to form 0D/3D CDs/ZFO composite photocatalyst was designed and synthetized via a self-templated solvothermal method. Compared with pure ZFO, the as-prepared CDs/ZFO composites displayed superior photocatalytic activity and stable for degradation of antibiotic pollutants under visible (420 < λ < 800 nm) and near infrared (λ > 800 nm) light irradiation. The corresponding mechanisms of degradation intermediates and photocatalytic reaction are further discussed in detail.

The CDs solution was synthetized via the electrochemical etching method [39]. Firstly, two graphite rods were served as anode and cathode and inserted into beaker containing a certain amount of deionized water. Then, a static potential of 30 V was applied in the graphite rod electrodes for 20 days. After the reaction, the obtained black CDs solution was filtered for several times, and treated in a freeze-drying way to collect the CDs solid powders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnFe2O4 (ZFO) and CDs/ZFO composites
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic process of CDs/ZFO was shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, 50 ml isopropanol and 10 ml glycerol were poured into 100 ml beaker and stirred on the magnetic stirring apparatus at 500 r·min-1. Meanwhile, 0.202 g Fe(NO)3 ·9H2O, 0.055 g Zn(CH3COO)2 and a certain amount of CDs solid powders were added into the above mixed solution and stirred for 30 min until uniform distribution. Then, the mixed precursor was transferred to the 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the sediment was washed with deionized water and alcohol several times, and dried at 60 °C for one night. Finally, the dried sample was put into crucible and heated at 350 °C (heating rate: 10 °C·min−1) for 120 min. The obtained samples were labeled as X% CDs/ZFO (X indicates the mass ratio of CDs to ZFO). The preparation process of pristine ZFO was similar to that of CDs/ZFO composite without adding CDs solid powders.

TC was selected as the main degradation antibiotic to detect the photocatalytic degradation performance of as-prepared samples. Other types of antibiotics, including amoxicillin (AMX), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and 7-aminocephalosporanic acid (7-ACA) were also applied in degradation process. The entire experimental process was carried out under 300 W Xenon lamp with λ > 420 nm (visible light) and λ > 800 nm (near-infrared) optical filters. First, 20 mg as-prepared photocatalyst was poured into quartz breaker containing 100 ml 30 mg·L−1 TC solution, and stirred in darkness for 30 min to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Then, the whole system was irradiated under visible/near-infrared light for 120 min, and 3 ml suspension was collected every 20 min. Finally, the extracted solution was centrifugated at 8000 r·min-1 and analyzed by UV–vis spectrophotometer.

During active free radicals capturing experimental process, vitamin C (VC), tertiary butanol (TBA) and triethanolamine (TEOA) were served as scavengers to capture superoxide radicals (
O2 −), hydroxy radicals (
OH) and holes (h+), respectively. The details of capturing experiments are consistent with the photocatalytic degradation experiments except for the addition of 1 mmol scavengers before the experimental process.
The detail characterizations were given in the Supplementary Material.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) technology was utilized to detect the crystal structure of as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 1 , the XRD pattern for ZFO is corresponding to the standard card of ZnFe2O4 (JPCDS 1-1109) [40]. The diffraction peaks located at 30.1°, 35.5°, 43.1°, 53.3°, 56.8° and 62.4° were attributed to the crystal reflection of (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes of ZnFe2O4, respectively [41]. The XRD pattern of 3% CDs/ZFO was similar to that of ZFO, indicating that the basic crystal structure of ZnFe2O4 was not changed after the introduction of CDs, and no obvious diffraction peak of CDs in 3% CDs/ZFO was observed, which is due to the low content and relatively weak diffraction intensity [42].

The Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectra of ZFO and 3% CDs/ZFO were displayed in Fig. 2 . For pure ZFO, the broad band located at 500–800 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of FeO/ZnO bands [43]. The other two characteristic peaks at 1107, 1395, 1634 and 3452 cm−1 are ascribed to bending and stretching of CO, CC, CO and OH, respectively [44]. As compared to ZFO, the FT-IR spectrum of 3% CDs/ZFO is similar, revealing that the fundamental structure of ZFO in the CDs/ZFO composite remains unchanged. Notably, it can be seen that the peak intensities of CC and CO in the 3% CDs/ZFO sample have been increased in the comparison of ZFO, suggesting the CDs were successfully loaded the surface of ZFO.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique was carried out to explore the surface chemical elemental compositions and states of ZFO and 3% CDs/ZFO. Through survey XPS spectra (Fig. S1(a)), the main elements in ZFO and 3% CDs/ZFO are Zn, Fe, O and C, and no other impurity element. In Fig. S1(b), due to the introduction of CDs, the intensity of C element peak in 3% CDs/ZFO is higher than that of ZFO. Moreover, high-resolution XPS spectra of each element are displayed in Fig. 3 . The spectrum of Zn 2p for ZFO can be divided into two peaks at 1021 and 1044.1 eV, which are attributed to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively [45]. The peaks in Fe 2p spectrum of ZFO at 710.2 and 712.2 eV are corresponded to Fe 2p3/2, and the peak at 724.6 eV is assigned to Fe 2p1/2 [46]. The two broad peaks located at 718 and 731.9 eV are belonged to the satellite peaks of Fe 2p [47]. In addition, the peaks in O 1s spectrum for ZFO located at 529.8 and 531 eV are originated from surface lattice oxygen containing ZnO/FeO bonds and the surface oxygen species [48], respectively. Compared with pure ZFO, high-resolution XPS spectra of each element for 3% CDs/ZFO are changed little, with only a small offset, suggesting possible chemical interactions and electron migration between ZFO and CDs [29]. The C 1s spectrum for 3% CDs/ZFO can be decomposed into three peaks located at 284.6, 285.9 and 288.4 eV are attributed to CC/CC, CO and CO bonds, respectively [49], revealing the existence of CDs in CDs/ZFO composite.

The microstructure of as-prepared sample was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As presented in Fig. 4 (a), it can be found that the 3% CDs/ZFO exhibits regular nanospheres structure with approximately 800–900 nm via SEM. Compared with SEM image of ZFO (Fig. S2(a)), there is no obvious change in microstructure after adding CDs in 3% CDs/ZFO, which may due to the lower content (3% (mass)) and smaller size (2–10 nm) of CDs in composite. Throughout TEM images of pure ZFO (Fig. S2(b)) and 3% CDs/ZFO (Fig. 4(b)), the unique yolk-shell were clearly observed, indicating that the incident light can be effectively reflected in inner structure to broaden the solar spectrum utilization range. To further confirm the existence of CDs, the HRTEM image of 3% CDs/ZFO was provided and shown in Fig. 4(c). It is seen that two different lattice stripes can be found where the lighter lattice stripe of 0.21 nm is attributed to the (100) crystal plane of CDs, and the dark lattice stripes of 0.25 nm is indexed to the (311) crystal plane of ZnFe2O4, demonstrating the successful construction of CDs/ZFO composite. The element mapping images (Fig. 4(d)) exhibited a homogeneous distribution of Zn, Fe, O and C elements, further suggesting a successful introduction of CDs on the surface of yolk-shell ZFO spheres.

As we know, absorbance of photocatalyst is a significant factor to influence photocatalytic activity, thus, the absorbance difference between ZFO and 3% CD/ZFO was detected using the UV-DRS technique. As exhibited in Fig. 5 (a), obviously, the absorption edge of 3% CDs/ZFO was similar to that of ZFO, but the absorbance of 3% CDs/ZFO in range of 300–600 nm and 700–800 nm was enhanced dramatically, which can attribute to the excellent absorption ability of CDs [50]. Moreover, the energy band gaps (E g) of ZFO was calculated via the Kubelka-Munk function and displayed in Fig. 5(b). It can be seen that the E g values of ZFO is calculated to be 1.94 eV. In order to further determine the energy band structure and position of pristine ZFO, the result of valance band XPS (VB-XPS) was exhibited in Fig. 5(c) and position of E VB of ZFO is 1.28 eV. Based on the above results, the position of E CB was calculated by the following formula [51]:
(1)
E
C B
=
E
V B
-
E g

Thus, the CB position of ZFO was −0.66 eV, and the corresponding energy band structure is given in Fig. 5(d). In addition, the up-converted PL spectra of CDs were tested. As shown in Fig. S3, the notable up-converted PL emission peaks at 540, 580 and 680 nm of CDs with excitations at long wavelengths of 700, 750 and 800 nm, respectively, indicating that CDs can be served as outstanding NIR light harvesters to enhance the NIR light utilization of semiconductor photocatalysts.

Tetracycline (TC) was served as target degradant to evaluate the photocatalytic degradation capability of as-prepared samples under visible and near-infrared light irradiation. As presented in Fig. 6 (a), the concentration of TC solution was unchanged under the condition with no photocatalyst in the visible light, which means that TC molecule can be stable in the aquatic environment and are not easy to photolysis. Moreover, the degradation efficiency of modified CDs/ZFO is significantly improved compared to pure ZFO (49% within 120 min). As the CDs contents increased from 0.5% to 3% in CDs/ZFO composites, the TC degradation efficiency increased from 67% to 78% within 120 min, while the CDs contents further increased to 10% and significantly decreased to 65%, which may be caused by the shading effect of CDs [29]. In order to determine the up-conversion property of CDs in as-prepared samples, the light source was linked with 800 nm cut-off filter before connecting the power and degradation curves are shown in Fig. 6(c). Pure ZFO exhibited poor photocatalytic degradation activity under near-infrared light due to the limited absorbance. In contrast, the CDs/ZFO displayed improved photocatalytic activity and 3% CDs/ZFO exhibited highest degradation efficiency (41% within 120 min). This is due to the excellent up-conversion ability of CDs that can modify ZFO, in turn for a wider absorption wavelength range. The corresponding degradation kinetic curves were exhibited in Fig. 6(b) and (d), and fitted by the following formula [52]:
(2)
- ln
C
C 0
= k t
where C (mg·L−1) is TC concentration in solution at time, C 0 (mg·L−1) is the initial TC concentration and k (min−1) is the reaction rate constant. Take for granted, the 3% CDs/ZFO possessed highest k value (0.0119 min−1 under visible light and 0.0026 min−1 under near-infrared light). Generally, drug wastewater contains different antibiotics that are difficult to decompose naturally. Therefore, we believe that the research concerning degradation rates for different types of antibiotics can provide more comprehensive response to practical potential of 3% CDs/ZFO. The degradation rates of amoxicillin (AMX), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and 7-aminocephalosporanic acid (7-ACA) were explored and displayed in Fig. S4 It can be seen that 3% CDs/ZFO photocatalyst shows a certain degradation effect on all of three antibiotics under visible and near-infrared light irradiation, and the highest degradation efficiency in AMX was over 50%. From these experimental results, it can be inferred that as-prepared CDs/ZFO composite possesses a great potential in full-scale applications. Cyclic stability is a critical factor to influence practical application of photocatalysts. In this work, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TC was remained over 70% after four cycles (Fig. 6(e)), indicating the excellent stability and sustainability for 3% CDs/ZFO. Furthermore, the magnetic recovery performance of photocatalyst is beneficial to reduce the loss of the samples during the process for recovery of sample. As exhibited in Fig. S5(a), the 3% CDs/ZFO solution is cloudy before contacting with magnet. Notably, the sample is fastened to the bottle wall after getting close to the magnet (Fig. S5(b) and (c)), indicating that 3% CDs/ZFO possesses outstanding magnetic recovery capability. Finally, the XRD patterns of 3% CDs/ZFO before and after photocatalysis are illustrated in Fig. 6(f). It can be seen that there is little difference between two XRD patterns, which was consistent with the results of the cycling experiment, revealing the excellent stability of CDs/ZFO prepared in this work.

Transient photocurrent response intensity with light on/off and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of photocatalyst are significant factors to reflect the transport efficiency of photo-generated carriers, and corresponding results are given in Fig. 7 . From Fig. 7(a), it is clearly that the photocurrent response of 3% CDs/ZFO is higher than that of ZFO, indicating that the faster transfer rate in electron-hole pairs and more utilized active groups in as-prepared composite. This phenomenon can attribute to the ability of CDs to easily collect and transfer electrons, thus improving the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs [53–58]. The EIS curves from Fig. 7(b) further denote that 3% CDs/ZFO possesses faster interfacial charge transfer with the smaller arc radius in comparison of pure ZFO.

Photoluminescence (PL) technology can effectively monitor the recombination rate of photo-generated carriers of photocatalyst [59–64]. In general, the high recombination rate between electrons and pairs corresponds to the high strength of PL. As displayed in Fig. 8 , the peak at around 470 nm is ascribed to the characteristic near-band-edge emission; while another peak may due to the defect-related emission [41]. Moreover, the PL intensity of 3% CDs/ZFO is lower than that of ZFO, indicating the faster separation and migration rate in electron-hole pairs of composite.

In order to explore free active radicals during photocatalytic degradation of TC, free-radical trap experiment was carried out and exhibited in Fig. 9 (a). In detail, vitamin C (VC), tert-butanol (TBA) and triethanolamine (TEOA) were used to capture superoxide radical (
O2 −), hydroxy-free radicals (
OH) and holes (h+), respectively. Compared with blank control experiment, all the TC degradation efficiencies are decreased after adding trapping agents, and the most reduced degradation efficiency was observed in solution with VC, meaning
O2 −,
OH and h+ are produced in degradation process, while the
O2 − radicals are the dominant active species in the CDs/ZFO photocatalytic system. Furthermore, electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments were also performed to detect
OH and
O2 − and the results are exhibited in Fig. 9(b). Under dark environment, neither DMPO-
OH nor DMPO-
O2 − signals can be observed, revealing the no active free radicals are produced without light. Until visible radiation for 10 min, the typical characteristic peaks for DMPO-
OH with 1:2:2:1 intensity ratio and strong signal peaks for DMPO-
O2 − can be distinctly detected.

Based on the above experimental results and analyses, a possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism over CDs/ZFO photocatalyst under visible and near infrared light irradiation is exhibited in Fig. 10 . Under visible light irradiation, the CDs/ZFO composite can be excited to produce photo-induced electrons, and these electrons would transfer to CB, meanwhile leaving h+ on the VB. After that, electrons (e−) would move from CB to CDs owing to the property of conducting electrons for CDs [65,66], which is beneficial to promote the separation and transfer in electron-hole pairs. Because of the position of CB for 3% CDs/ZFO (−0.89 eV) is more negative than conversion potential between O2 and
O2 − (−0.33 eV) [52], electrons on CB have reduced O2 into
O2 − radicals directly. Nevertheless, the position of VB for 3% CDs/ZFO is 1.05 eV, which also negative than conversion potential between
OH and H2O (2.4 eV) [67], thus h+ on VB cannot react with H2O to produce
OH, but degrade TC directly due to the strong oxidizing property of holes. And the
OH involved in photocatalytic degradation process may generated by the following routes [68]:
(3)
O2 − + H+ →
HO2
(4) e− +
HO2 → HO2 −
(5)
H
O
2
-
+
H
+
→
H 2
O 2
(6) e− + H2O2 →
OH + OH−

Furthermore, under near-infrared light irradiation, the photons cannot be absorbed due to the limited absorbance range of the CFO-light catalyst. Notably, benefiting from up-conversion property of CDs, the long-wavelength light (λ > 800 nm) can be converted to shorter-wavelength (420 < λ < 800 nm) via CDs [69], thus stimulating CFO to generate photo-induced carriers, further realizing the photocatalytic degradation of TC reaction under near-infrared light.

The possible intermediates during TC wastewater degradation process for 3% CDs/ZFO composite were explored by liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS) technology and corresponding spectra are shown in Fig. S6. Meanwhile, based on previous reports concerning degradation intermediates of TC, the possible degradation routes were analyzed and displayed in Fig. 11 . For path I, Product 1 (P1, m/z = 418) was formed due to the N-methyl and amino group separated from TC, and P2 (m/z = 399) was formed via the dehydration reaction [70]. Furthermore, P3 (m/z = 228) and P4 (m/z = 192) were generated from P2 owing to breaking of CC/CC bond and removal of methyl groups [71]. For path II, the generation of P5 (m/z = 417) may be ascribed to the loss of N-methyl in TC. Subsequently, P5 (m/z = 417) was fragmented into P6 (m/z = 340) and P7 (m/z = 227) via leaving the group of CO [72]. Then, P4 and P8 were translated into some small molecular weight product due to the participation of active radicals, such as P8 (m/z = 75) and P9 (m/z = 143). Finally, all the intermediates would be degraded into CO2 and H2O, etc. In order to detect the biological toxicity between TC solution before and after photocatalytic degradation, we have performed mung bean sprouts planting experiments in three different growing environments: (i) pure water; (ii) TC solution (1 ml TC solution + 14 ml pure water) and (iii) TC solution after degradation (1 ml TC solution after degradation + 14 ml pure water). In the typical experimental process, 15 uniform and plump mung beans were put in three culture dishes with these growing environments respectively. The planting experiment was performed under dark conditions, and the culture solution was replaced every 24 h. The growth of bean sprouts after 6 days was exhibited in Fig. S7. Obviously, all of bean sprouts grown in pure water were germinated and displayed excellent growth condition (Fig. S7(a)). Growth conditions grown in TC solution after photocatalytically degraded were similar to those grown in pure water, but one mung bean did not sprout (Fig. S7(b)). Clearly, bean sprouts grown in TC solution had poor growth conditions compared to the other two growth environments, and the germination rate was not only reduced, but also in poor growth status (Fig. S7(c)). Based on the results of the biological planting experiments, the biological toxicity of TC solution after photocatalysis was reduced dramatically, indicating that as-prepared photocatalyst could possess superior ability of photocatalytic degradation of TC and further reduce the toxicity of TC to organisms.

In summary, CDs modified yolk-shell ZFO composite photocatalysts have been successfully synthetized via a self-templated solvothermal method. Under visible and near infrared light irradiation, the as-prepared CDs/ZFO composite displayed excellent photocatalytic degradation performance and stability, which may be ascribed to the following two reasons: (i) the incident light can be effectively multiple reflected in the unique yolk-shell structure for ZFO to increase the utilization efficiency of incident light; (ii) electronic conductivity and up-conversion photoluminescence properties of CDs effectively improve separation and transfer of electron-hole pairs and broaden the utilization range of solar spectrum of ZFO. Based on the electron spin resonance (ESR) and active free radical capture experiments, superoxide radicals (
O2 −) are determined as the dominant active group during photocatalytic process. This study provides an idea of designing and synthesizing effective near-infrared photocatalysts, which may be important for future environmental restoration.

Zhongyi Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Feng Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Lijing Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
